workflows.title: AI workshop intake template for teams
workflows.problem: You're running an internal AI workshop and don't know what each attendee actually wants to build, so the session ends up generic.
workflows.target_query: AI workshop intake template for teams
workflows.outcome: Each attendee shows up with a specific workflow problem, sample inputs, and a publish-or-not decision before the workshop starts.
tools: Any LLM with a draft-only review pass
workflows.time: 30 minutes to send · 1 hour to triage 20 responses
steps: Define the workshop's single outcome — a published workflow, a working draft, or a reviewed prompt.
Send an intake message asking: role, one workflow they want, sample input they can share safely, what they'd never automate.
Use an LLM in draft-only mode to cluster responses into 3-5 themes. Read every response yourself.
Pre-write a starter workflow for each cluster so no one starts at a blank screen.
Confirm with each attendee 24 hours before the session that the starter fits their problem.
workflows.example_output: Cluster: Founder weekly metrics. 4 attendees. Starter: AI meeting-notes-to-action-tracker workflow. Sample input: anonymized weekly review notes.
workflows.human_review: An organizer must read every intake reply before clustering. LLM may suggest themes but cannot decide who attends or what is exposed.
workflows.privacy_notes: Do not paste real customer messages, health, or financial data into the LLM. Use sanitized examples or schemas only.
fork_ideas: Use the same intake for community-bot onboarding interviews.
Use it as a pre-newsletter survey to seed practical issues.
Adapt for academic course intake — replace 'workflow' with 'research question'.

workflows.title: WhatsApp community onboarding bot workflow
workflows.problem: Every new community member asks the same 5 questions and you answer them at 11pm.
workflows.outcome: New members get a consistent welcome, a clear path to the next event, and your phone stays calm.
workflows.time: 2 hours to draft · 1 week of human review before going live
steps: Write the 5 questions you actually answer most weeks — exact phrasings, not paraphrases.
Write canonical answers in the community's voice. Each answer must say what the human admin will do next.
Configure the bot to respond only to message patterns you've reviewed. New patterns escalate to the human.
Run a dry-run week where the bot proposes replies to the human admin before sending.
Go live with a clearly stated 'I'm the community AI host, an admin will jump in if needed' opener.
workflows.example_output: New member asks 'when is next session?' Bot replies with the upcoming session, RSVP link, and tags admin if member asks anything off-script.
workflows.human_review: Human admin must approve every new response pattern for the first 14 days. AI never decides who joins, who leaves, or moderation actions.
workflows.privacy_notes: Do not log raw member messages with phone numbers attached. Store only event types and counts. Never send member content to a third-party LLM without redaction.
fork_ideas: Use for resident communities, alumni groups, or product-user channels.
Add an event RSVP capture flow.
Add a weekly digest summarizing pending admin questions.

workflows.title: Founder weekly metrics build-in-public workflow
workflows.problem: You want to share weekly progress publicly but don't know which numbers honestly reflect momentum versus vanity.
workflows.target_query: founder weekly metrics build in public workflow
workflows.outcome: Each Friday you publish a 6-line update with the same metrics, plus one lesson, in 30 minutes.
steps: Pick 6 metrics: visitors, returning visitors, signups, replies/conversations, workflow submissions or product actions, and MRR (or $0 if true).
Add 2 qualitative lines: what experiment ran, what surprised you.
Add 1 explicit failure or thing that didn't work.
Use an LLM to compress the raw notes into the same 6-line template every week.
Publish on the same day and time every week so readers know when to expect it.
workflows.example_output: Week of 2026-05-26 — visitors 480, returning 92, newsletter signups 14, workflow submissions 3, MRR $0. Tried X reply-guy rotation; got 1 newsletter swap conversation. Failed: assumed Indie Hackers post would convert; it brought 0 signups.
workflows.human_review: Founder writes the failure line themselves. LLM may rephrase but must not invent metrics.
workflows.privacy_notes: Do not publish member-level data, individual phone numbers, or private partner names without consent.
fork_ideas: Adapt for a course/cohort weekly update.
Adapt for an open-source project (commits, contributors, issues).
Add a 'what I'm asking for help with' line to recruit collaborators.

workflows.title: AI meeting notes to action tracker workflow
workflows.problem: Meeting recordings pile up. Decisions and owners get lost the next morning.
workflows.target_query: AI meeting notes to action tracker workflow
workflows.outcome: Every meeting ends with a confirmed action list owned by named humans before anyone leaves the call.
steps: Capture the transcript locally. Strip names of anyone outside the company before sending to a third-party LLM.
Ask the LLM for a strict format: Decisions, Action items (owner + due), Open questions. No prose.
Read the list out loud at the end of the meeting and confirm each owner. Edit live.
Paste the confirmed list into the task tracker as separate items with owners.
After the meeting, archive the transcript privately or delete it per your retention rule.
workflows.example_output: Decisions: Move launch to 2026-06-08. Actions: Boogi — finalize newsletter copy by 2026-06-02. Open: Do we need a Substack swap before launch?
workflows.human_review: Human owners must verbally confirm every action item. LLM cannot assign owners on its own.
workflows.privacy_notes: Never paste customer health, financial, legal, or HR content into a public LLM. Use a local model or redact first.
fork_ideas: Add a 'risks raised' section for ops/legal meetings.
Adapt for 1-on-1s with a 'commitments' line on both sides.
Pipe outputs into the build-in-public weekly update.

workflows.title: Research paper summarization workflow with human review
workflows.problem: You need to triage 30 papers a week but can't read them all, and AI summaries hallucinate citations.
workflows.target_query: research paper summarization workflow with human review
workflows.outcome: You get a 5-line summary, claim-level citations, and a 'should I read it' verdict in 4 minutes per paper — with hallucinated claims caught before they enter your notes.
workflows.time: 4 minutes per paper · 20 minutes per batch
steps: Define the 5 fields: claim, method, sample size, result, your relevance note.
Feed the paper to the LLM only after stripping sensitive subject data (where applicable).
Ask the LLM to extract claims with quoted source spans, not paraphrases.
Spot-check at least 3 claims against the paper itself. If any fails, discard the summary.
Save only verified claims into your notes — never the raw LLM output.
workflows.example_output: Claim: 'Method X improves recall by 12%' [p.4, ¶3]. Method: replication study. Sample: 1,200 docs. Result: confirmed within 1%. Relevance: relevant for our retrieval workflow.
workflows.human_review: Reviewer must verify each claim's source span before saving. LLM may not assign novelty, citation count, or topical importance.
workflows.privacy_notes: Do not feed unpublished, embargoed, or human-subjects data to a third-party LLM. Use a local model or redact.
fork_ideas: Adapt for grant-proposal triage.
Adapt for journalism source review.
Pair with a weekly lit-review newsletter.

workflows.title: Healthcare ops call triage workflow with safety caveats
workflows.problem: Front-desk calls mix appointment requests, billing questions, and urgent symptoms — and untrained routing risks safety.
workflows.target_query: healthcare ops call triage workflow with safety caveats
workflows.outcome: Non-urgent calls are routed and logged quickly; anything that smells urgent goes immediately to a human clinician — never to AI.
workflows.time: 1 week to draft, 4 weeks to validate before any live use
steps: Have a clinician define a 'never automate' list: chest pain, suicidal ideation, pregnancy bleeding, pediatric fever, allergic reaction, anything emergency-coded by your jurisdiction.
Build a routing tree that only handles 3-4 known non-clinical categories: appointment scheduling, billing, hours, directions.
Anything outside the tree, including any of the 'never automate' phrases, must immediately transfer to a human.
Run a shadow mode where AI suggests routing but a human approves every decision for 4 weeks.
Publish a public disclosure that AI assists with non-clinical routing only and that members can opt out of AI handling.
workflows.example_output: Caller says 'I want to reschedule Tuesday' → AI routes to scheduling. Caller says 'I'm having trouble breathing' → AI immediately transfers to human + flags audit log.
workflows.human_review: A clinician must own this workflow. AI cannot triage symptoms, give medical advice, or override the 'never automate' list. All AI decisions are auditable.
workflows.privacy_notes: PHI must never be sent to a third-party LLM without a signed BAA. Do not log call audio without explicit consent. Comply with your jurisdiction's privacy law.
fork_ideas: Adapt for legal-aid call triage with similar 'never automate' lists.
Adapt for school front-desk calls about safety incidents.
Use the same 'never automate' pattern for any safety-critical inbox.

primary_cta.label: Talk to us before deploying this

workflows.title: Creator content repurposing workflow
workflows.problem: You publish one good piece a week, but it dies on the page — and rewriting it for every channel sounds like a different person wrote it.
workflows.outcome: One published piece becomes a newsletter, a thread, a video script outline, and a community discussion seed — all in your voice.
tools: A voice sample (3-5 of your own posts)
steps: Lock the source piece. Don't repurpose drafts.
Build a voice prompt from 3-5 of your own posts: sentence length, vocabulary you do and don't use, lines you'd never write.
Generate channel drafts one at a time — newsletter, thread, video outline, community seed.
Read each draft out loud. If a sentence isn't yours, rewrite or delete it.
Schedule across the week so the original piece keeps earning attention.
workflows.example_output: Source: blog post on practical AI workshops. Output: 1 newsletter intro, 1 X thread (6 tweets), 1 video outline (5 beats), 1 community discussion question.
workflows.human_review: The creator reads every output aloud and edits before posting. LLM may not auto-publish.
workflows.privacy_notes: Don't feed unpublished client work or private feedback into a third-party LLM as 'voice samples'. Use your own public writing.
fork_ideas: Add a 'what I'd never publish' line to filter outputs.
Adapt for a podcast → newsletter → thread loop.
Adapt for translating content into another language while preserving voice.

workflows.title: No-code lead qualification workflow
workflows.problem: Inbound leads include great fits, casual browsers, and time-wasters — and you waste hours sorting them.
workflows.outcome: Every inbound lead lands with a clear 'reach out today / next week / never' label and an explainer line before you read it.
tools: A no-code automation runner of your choice
workflows.time: 3 hours to wire · 5 minutes per day to review
steps: Define 'great fit' in 5 hard criteria: role, company size, budget signal, urgency wording, exclusion rule.
Have an LLM score new leads against the criteria — output: tier (now/soon/never) + 1-line reason.
Send tier + reason into your CRM/spreadsheet, never the raw LLM rationale prose.
Spot-check 20% of 'never' classifications every week for false negatives.
Retire any criterion that doesn't change a decision after 30 days.
workflows.example_output: Lead: ops manager, mid-size healthcare ops team, mentions burnout from manual call routing. Tier: now. Reason: clinical-adjacent ops + urgency wording.
workflows.human_review: Founder/owner reviews 'now' tier same-day and a 20% sample of 'never' tier weekly. AI never auto-emails leads.
workflows.privacy_notes: Strip personal contact info before sending lead content to a third-party LLM, or use a local model. Do not store free-text lead messages outside your CRM.
fork_ideas: Adapt for inbound partnership requests.
Adapt for job applications with explicit 'never use for protected categories' rules.
Adapt for community-bot upgrade requests.

workflows.title: Customer support FAQ gap-mining workflow
workflows.problem: Your FAQ answers what you assumed users would ask, not what they're actually asking — and the gap shows up as support load.
workflows.target_query: customer support FAQ gap mining workflow
workflows.outcome: Every week you publish 3 new FAQ answers grounded in real questions that didn't have an answer yet.
tools: Last week's support tickets or chat logs
steps: Export last week's support questions with PII stripped (no names, no emails, no phone numbers, no IDs).
Ask the LLM to cluster questions into themes and list the top 5 themes with example wording.
Compare to existing FAQ. Flag themes with no current answer.
Write the 3 highest-impact new FAQ entries yourself, not the LLM. Use exact user wording in the question.
Publish, and add a 'last updated' date to each entry.
workflows.example_output: Theme 1: 'Can the bot pause replies during off-hours?' — 8 mentions, no FAQ entry. New FAQ: 'Yes, see the off-hours pause toggle in the host config.'
workflows.human_review: Human writes every FAQ answer. LLM only clusters and counts. No automated FAQ publishing.
workflows.privacy_notes: Never include customer names, emails, phone numbers, or specific case details. Use 'a member asked' phrasing.
fork_ideas: Adapt for internal HR Q&A.
Adapt for an academic course FAQ.
Adapt for a community FAQ refreshed weekly.

workflows.title: AI tool evaluation scorecard workflow
workflows.problem: New AI tools ship every week. You can't evaluate them all, and bad tool choices cost months.
workflows.outcome: Every tool gets a 30-minute scorecard with a keep/park/kill verdict and a follow-up date — no more open tabs forever.
tools: Sample inputs from your real workflows
An LLM only as a comparison aid
steps: Define 6 fields: real problem it solves, your sample input, output quality, integration cost, privacy posture, switching cost.
Try the tool on your real (sanitized) sample input — not the vendor's demo data.
Score each field 1-5. No averages — just look at the worst score.
Decide: keep (use this month), park (revisit in 90 days), kill (don't reopen). Set the date.
Publish the scorecard internally so you don't re-evaluate the same tool in 30 days.
workflows.example_output: Tool: Vendor X note-taker. Worst score: privacy posture (2/5, no clear data-retention policy). Verdict: park. Revisit: 2026-08-26.
workflows.human_review: Founder/team lead writes the verdict and date. LLM may compare features but cannot decide adoption.
workflows.privacy_notes: Never paste real customer data, employee data, or unreleased product information into vendor tools during evaluation. Use sanitized samples.
fork_ideas: Adapt for hiring tool evaluation.
Adapt for community-bot platform comparison.
Adapt for picking a model provider with clear privacy rules.
